title: Love One Another Because of the Gospel
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 10-15-23 -PM- Love One Another Because of the Gospel - 1 Peter 1_22-25 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Love for the Brethren as a Repeating Theme in 1 Peter
subpoints: Peter mentions love for the brethren in chapters 1, 2, 3, and 4 — it is one of the dominant themes of the letter
Main action of the passage: verse 22 — "Love one another fervently with a pure heart"
Everything else in the passage explains this main action and gives two reasons for it

outline.title: Reason 1: We Have Purified Souls — Sanctification Produces Brotherly Love (v. 22)
subpoints: "Since you have purified your souls in obeying the truth through the Spirit in sincere love of the brethren" — sanctification results in love for the brethren
Growing in obedience to the truth naturally produces growing love for one another; the two cannot be separated
Hatred or indifference for the brethren is a sign of immaturity and lack of faith; any doctrine that consistently leads to hatred should be questioned as to whether it is biblical

outline.title: Reason 2: We Have Been Born Again — Regeneration Produces Brotherly Love (vv. 23-25)
subpoints: "Having been born again, not of corruptible seed but incorruptible, through the word of God" — the new birth as cause of love for the brethren
Jeremiah 31 new covenant promise: the renewed heart naturally loves those who belong to God's covenant community
Peter writes "having been born again" in the perfect tense — we were born again in the past and continue in that state; it does not change, and therefore love for the brethren is permanent
If a life is marked by hatred or bitterness toward brothers and sisters, one should question whether a regenerate heart is present
The Word of God (logos = written Word, rhema = spoken Word) is both living and abiding — it does not stop working and it never goes away; God uses both reading and preaching to regenerate
Isaiah 40:6-8 quoted to prove the enduring nature of the Word: flesh is grass and fades; the Word of the Lord endures forever
Verse 25 concludes: this very enduring Word is the gospel that was preached to them

outline.title: The Goal: Growing from Brotherly Love to Fervent Love
subpoints: Peter uses two different Greek words for love: the first in verse 22 ("sincere love of the brethren") and a stronger word ("love one another fervently") — indicating a progression
As we grow in grace, our love for one another is to ramp up — from brotherly love to fervent, self-sacrificing love
John 13:34-35: "As I have loved you, love one another" — the pattern is Christ's own self-giving sacrifice; "no greater love than to lay down one's life for one's friends"
1 Thessalonians 5:14: admonish the idle, encourage the fainthearted, help the weak, be patient with all

Congregational examples of fervent love: praying for one another, doing dishes, showing up for church, having difficult conversations with grace, forgiving, financial help, hospitality, asking how someone is and actually waiting to hear, visiting those who cannot get out

summary:
Preaching from 1 Peter 1:22–25 in the evening service, Pastor Tito Lyro focuses on the foundational reason the New Testament repeatedly commands believers to love one another. The main action of the passage is verse 22's command: "Love one another fervently with a pure heart." Peter supplies two reasons why Christians can and must do this, both rooted in the gospel.

The first reason is sanctification: as we obey the truth through the Spirit, our souls are purified, and the natural fruit of that obedience is genuine love for the brethren. Hatred or indifference is not merely a moral failing — it is evidence of stunted faith and immaturity. The second reason is the new birth: the same Word of God that is both living (never stops working) and abiding (never goes away) was used by God to regenerate our hearts, and that new, regenerate heart necessarily loves those who also belong to the covenant community. Peter quotes Isaiah 40:6–8 to prove the Word's endurance — flesh is grass and fades, but the Word of the Lord endures forever, and that enduring Word is the very gospel preached to them.

Peter's goal is not merely brotherly love but fervent love — he uses two progressively stronger Greek words for love, signaling that as we grow in grace, love for the brethren must grow with it. The pattern is Christ's own self-sacrifice (John 13:34–35). The sermon closes with an interactive discussion with the congregation identifying concrete expressions of fervent love: prayer, helping carry things into church, hospitality, forgiving, financial help, visiting the housebound, and asking how someone is — and actually waiting to hear the answer.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading — 1 Peter 1:22–25

Since you have purified your souls in obeying the truth through the Spirit in sincere love of the brethren, love one another fervently with a pure heart, having been born again, not of corruptible seed but incorruptible, through the word of God which lives and abides forever, because all flesh is as grass, and all the glory of man as the flower of the grass. The grass withers, and its flower falls away, but the word of the Lord endures forever. Now this is the word which by the gospel was preached to you.

Let us pray. Father, we thank you for your word. We pray that it would be faithfully proclaimed and that you would use it, implanted in our hearts, that we might live according to what you have for us. We pray in Jesus' name. Amen.

## Introduction

Virtually every book of the New Testament includes some sort of exhortation to love one another, and Peter is no exception to that. He mentions it for the first time here in the passage we just read, but then he comes to it again in chapter 2, chapter 3, and chapter 4 — so only chapter 5 is somewhat missing it. In our passage he gives us the foundational reason why we should love one another.

The main action of this passage is found in verse 22, where Peter says: "Love one another fervently with a pure heart." That is the main point of the passage. And everything else explains this main action and gives us two reasons why we can do that and why we should do that.

## Reason 1: We Have Purified Souls (v. 22)

The first reason for fervent love of the brethren is that we have purified souls, in verse 22: "Since you have purified your souls in obeying the truth through the Spirit in sincere love of the brethren, love one another." Peter's point here is that sanctification results in love for the brethren. Our souls have been purified and continue to be purified as we obey the truth, and the natural result of obedience is love for the brethren. One does not exist without the other. As we grow in obedience to the truth, we grow in brotherly love for one another.

So hatred or indifference for the brethren is a sign of immaturity, a sign of lack of growth, a sign of lack of faith in Jesus Christ. So if we are experiencing hatred or even indifference toward our brethren, it is because we are not growing in the truth of the Lord Jesus Christ. And if a doctrine consistently leads to hatred for the brethren or even indifference to the brethren, we should question whether that doctrine is from the Bible. Peter says here that we have been purified and continue to be purified by obedience to the truth, and that results in brotherly love.

## Reason 2: We Have Been Born Again (vv. 23–25)

The second reason is in verse 23: "Having been born again, not of corruptible seed but incorruptible, through the word of God which lives and abides forever." Peter says: you are going to love the brethren because you are growing in sanctification — you have purified your souls and continue to purify them by obedience to the truth. But then he goes further back than our sanctification to speak about the new birth as a reason for why we should love the brethren.

He tells us that God has changed our hearts by the power of His word, and because of that we love the brethren. Love of the brethren is a necessary result of having experienced the new birth in Christ. It is a necessary result of having had your heart changed by the Spirit of God. You have been given a heart of flesh that is able to believe. If you were to go back to Jeremiah 31 where God is talking about the new covenant — the renewal of the heart — one of the things that comes from that renewed heart is a loving relationship with others who belong to God's covenant community. It is of the essence of the new covenant that if you have had your heart changed, you love those whom God loves as well.

He writes the clause "having been born again" in a way that says we have been born again in the past and we continue in the state of having been born again. This does not change, and therefore we love the brethren. There is no facet of the Christian life that does not include loving the brethren. As long as you are in a state of having been born again — which we believe never changes, because we believe the saints persevere to the end — you must love the brethren. It is natural to your life.

So if you have a hard time loving the brethren, it is fair for you to question whether you have a new, regenerate heart. If your life is marked by hatred, by bitterness toward other brothers and sisters in your life, you need to look at your heart, because love for the brethren is a result of regeneration.

He says that the cause of the new birth is the Word of God, which is both living — meaning it does not stop working — and abiding, meaning it remains; it does not go away with us. And that living, abiding word is what God uses to regenerate the person, to give us a new heart. It is a mysterious thing, because the new birth is not a response we have in us; it is something that happens to us — but it happens as the Word of God is read or proclaimed. It is a matter of fact that Peter uses two different words for "word" in this passage: *logos* to indicate the written Word of God and *rhema* to indicate the spoken Word of God. Both the reading of God's Word and the preaching of God's Word lead to the new birth, which in turn leads to love for the brethren.

Peter quotes Isaiah 40:6–8 to prove the enduring nature of God's Word as it produces the new birth and obedience. Look at verses 24–25: "All flesh is as grass, and all the glory of man as the flower of the grass. The grass withers, and its flower falls away, but the word of the Lord endures forever." Mankind comes and goes, but the Word of God remains. And as long as the Word of God remains, hearts will be changed and the brethren will be loved. And at the end of verse 25 Peter says this very Word is the Word that was preached to them as the gospel of Jesus Christ.

## From Brotherly Love to Fervent Love

Peter says that these two reasons — we are growing in obedience, and we have been born again — are why we love the brethren fervently (verse 22). It is interesting that Peter wants them to move beyond general brotherly love to fervent love, to supreme love. He uses two different words for love here to indicate that progression. The changing word in verse 22 — from sincere love of the brethren to "love one another fervently" — tells us that as we grow in obedience to the truth, as we are born again, our love for one another ramps up. As we grow in the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, our love for one another also grows with it.

This is a repeating theme in the letter. Peter repeats it in chapter 2 verse 11: "Honor all people; love the brotherhood; fear God; honor the king." He repeats it again in chapter 3 verse 8: "Finally, all of you be of one mind, having compassion for one another, love as brothers, be tenderhearted, be courteous." And for the last time he mentions it in chapter 4 verse 8: "And above all things have fervent love for one another, for love will cover a multitude of sins."

And Paul is also on board with this idea. In Romans 12:10 he says: "Be kindly affectionate to one another with brotherly love, in honor giving preference to one another."

Jesus Himself helps us understand the nature of this love in John 13:34–35: "A new commandment I give to you, that you love one another — as I have loved you, that you also love one another. By this all will know that you are My disciples, if you have love for one another." How has Christ loved us? He loved us by giving, by sacrificing. So the perfect love we are to have for one another is a self-sacrificing love, a self-giving love. That is the pattern our Savior gives us. And then later in John: "No greater love has anyone than this, to lay down one's life for his friends."

Now I want to see if we could go around and think of examples of how this looks in practice. How does loving one another fervently with self-giving, self-sacrificing love look in practice?

*[Congregation participates: praying for one another; doing dishes after lunch; showing up for church — encouraging each other in faithfulness; having difficult conversations that need to be had, with grace; forgiving — the most costly form of love, giving up the right to punish; financial help; physically helping when there is need; having people over — hospitality; asking how someone is doing and actually waiting to hear the answer; teaching Sunday school and Kids Club; giving hugs of welcome; visiting people who cannot get out.]*

Paul gives us a pattern in 1 Thessalonians 5:14: "We urge you, brothers, admonish the idle, encourage the fainthearted, help the weak, be patient with all." That is a great way to finish — those are concrete ways we can actually love the brethren with fervent love. It is not the super-Christian thing to do; it is the Christian thing to do. It is not for the super-spiritual — it is for anyone who has been born of God.

## Conclusion

We have been purified and continue to be purified. We have been born again and continue in that state. So what do we do? We love one another fervently with clean hearts, and we drive each other to Christ by a demonstration of the very love that Christ had for us as we give ourselves and sacrifice ourselves for each other.

Let us pray together. Father, we thank you that you are good to us. We thank you that the Lord Jesus Christ has not only taught us about love but showed what love is. Help us to follow Him in that, out of pure hearts, out of regenerated hearts, that we might love the brethren fervently. We ask in Jesus' name. Amen.
